title: Keeping quail on a smallholding
subtitle: Small birds, fast returns: eggs in six weeks and a colony that runs well in a shed or run.
excerpt: Quail mature faster than any other poultry. A hen begins laying at five to six weeks old, produces up to 300 eggs a year, and needs a fraction of the space a chicken flock requires. A guide to housing, feeding, and managing a small laying colony for egg production.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Gamebirds Reared for Sporting Purposes (Annex: quail welfare), Crown copyright. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive. University extension service open-access factsheets on Coturnix quail production.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Japanese quail (Coturnix japonica), the standard domestic egg-laying and meat quail breed. Fast-maturing, easy to manage, and widely available as day-old chicks or hatching eggs in the UK.
The number of birds per unit of floor area. Overstocking quail causes feather-pecking, stress, and disease. The recommended maximum is 6 hens per square metre of floor space.

content.text: Coturnix quail are the most productive small poultry for a smallholding. A hen starts laying at five to six weeks old and produces eggs weighing around 10 g each through most of the year. A colony of 20 hens takes up less floor space than a single chicken, costs less to feed, and produces eggs that retail well at farmers' markets.

can be kept in a shed with a wire floor, a colony rabbit hutch, or a purpose-built quail cage. The key requirement is a solid roof. Quail fly straight up when startled and can stun or kill themselves against an open-topped pen. Cover the top of any run completely, and keep the height under 45 cm to reduce the force of any upward collision.
at or below six hens per square metre. A 1 m by 1 m pen holds a colony of six comfortably with room for feeders and drinkers. Overcrowded quail feather-peck within days.

attrs.title: Wire floor or litter floor?
attrs.body: A wire floor with 12 mm mesh lets droppings fall clear, keeps the birds cleaner, and reduces disease. A litter floor using wood shavings is warmer in winter but needs more frequent changing. For small egg-laying colonies in a temperate UK climate, wire floor is more practical.

content.text: Quail need a higher protein ration than chickens: 20 to 24 percent protein for laying hens versus the 15 to 16 percent of standard layer pellets. Use a turkey starter or grower crumb for the first six weeks, then switch to a quail layer crumb or a gamebird layer if quail-specific feed is not locally available. Do not use standard chicken layer pellets as the sole ration; protein content is too low for consistent production.

A small gravity feeder hung at back-level works well. Quail scatter feed aggressively by flicking their beaks through it; a feeder with a lip or guard reduces waste. Change feed daily if the feeder is on the small side; stale or damp feed grows mould quickly and quail will stop eating it.

Water must be available at all times. A shallow drinker with a narrow trough works better than an open dish, which quail wade in and foul. Check drinkers twice daily in hot weather; quail dehydrate rapidly above 30 degrees.

For an egg-laying colony, a ratio of one cock to four or five hens reduces fighting among males while keeping hens fertile if you want to hatch eggs. For a non-breeding laying colony, all hens with no cock is practical; unfertilised eggs are identical in taste and nutrition.

Quail do not perch or use nest boxes. They lay directly on the floor or wire. Collect eggs twice daily. Quail laid on a wire floor tend to be cleaner than those laid on litter; wipe any that are soiled with a slightly damp cloth and dry before storing.

Day-old quail chicks are tiny and need immediate warmth at 35 degrees for the first week, reducing by 5 degrees each week until they are fully feathered at around four weeks. A brooder heat plate set to its lowest position and raised gradually works well. Chicks that pile on top of each other are too cold; chicks that huddle at the edges away from the plate are too hot.

items.symptom: Hens laying well initially then production drops sharply
items.cause: Day length shortening in autumn, or dietary protein too low
items.fix: Add supplementary lighting to maintain 14 to 16 hours of light per day. Check the protein content of the feed and switch to a higher-protein ration if using standard layer pellets.

items.symptom: Feather-pecking and bald patches on hens
items.cause: Overcrowding, boredom, or too high a cock-to-hen ratio
items.fix: Reduce stocking density immediately. Remove any birds that are causing injury. Check that the pen has adequate floor space and that feeders are not causing competition.

items.symptom: Chicks dying in the first three days
items.cause: Chilling, dehydration, or feed not found
items.fix: Check brooder temperature with a thermometer. Dip each chick's beak in the drinker on arrival to teach them where water is. Sprinkle a little feed directly on the floor for the first 24 hours so they find it easily.
